title: Transcription and RNA processing
description: Test your knowledge of transcription and RNA processing!

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows a double-stranded DNA molecule.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following represents the correct mRNA molecule that will result from the transcription of the DNA molecule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To produce an mRNA molecule during transcription, RNA polymerase uses the *noncoding* strand of the template DNA molecule.

RNA polymerase synthesizes the mRNA molecule in the 5’ to 3’ direction while reading the noncoding DNA strand in the 3’ to 5’ direction. Nucleotides are added to the growing mRNA molecule according to base-pairing rules, where U is paired with A, A is paired with T, and C and G are paired with each other.

[[☃ image 1]]

So, the correct answer is

[[☃ image 3]]

options.alt: Two strands of DNA are shown joined by nucleotide bases. The strand on the left is 3 prime at the top and 5 prime at the bottom, and is labeled coding strand. The base sequence on the coding strand is A, T, G, C, G, A, C, T. The strand on the right is 5 prime at the top and 3 prime at the bottom, and is labeled non-coding strand. The base sequence on the non-coding strand is T, A, C, G, C, T, G, A.

choices.content: ![A strand of RNA runs from 5 prime to 3 prime and has the base sequence U, C, A, G, C, G, U, A.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/156e8256d56b89963436c7e7d0e0985f.png)
![A strand of RNA runs from 5 prime to 3 prime and has the base sequence A, U, G, C, G, A, C, U.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3f1bbceeff0b9f344836aded3eb03093.png)
![A strand of RNA runs from 5 prime to 3 prime and has the base sequence U, A, C, G, C, U, G, A.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/872c0ce6f348a4ef8746b01905351817.png)
![A strand of RNA runs from 5 prime to 3 prime and has the base sequence A, G, U, C, G, C, A, U.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/79506710d941452f40a1f82f61646465.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram represents the flow of genetic information from DNA to protein.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following describes an event that occurs during Step 3 in the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A series of events begins with DNA with an arrow pointing to Pre mRNA and labeled step 1. Pre mRNA points to mRNA and is labeled step 2 and the mRNA points to protein and is labeled step 3.

choices.content: tRNAs are used to add amino acids to a growing polypeptide chain.
mRNAs are produced by RNA polymerase using a template molecule.
rRNAs are translated into proteins in the cytosol.
mRNAs are processed to contain only certain exons.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram represents the flow of genetic information from DNA to protein.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following describes an event that occurs during Step 1 in the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A series of events begins with DNA with an arrow pointing to Pre mRNA and labeled step 1. Pre mRNA points to mRNA and is labeled step 2 and then mRNA points to protein and is labeled step 3.

choices.content: pre-mRNAs are produced by RNA polymerase using a template molecule.
pre-mRNAs are processed to include only certain exons.
tRNAs and rRNAs are used to add amino acids to a growing polypeptide chain.
mRNAs are translated into pre-mRNAs in the cytosol.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram represents the flow of genetic information from DNA to protein.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following describes an event that occurs during Step 2 in the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A flow diagram. In step 1 D N A  shows an arrow pointing to pre-m R N A. In step 2, pre-m R N A shows an arrow pointing to m R N A. In step 3, m R N A shows an arrow pointing to protein.

choices.content: pre-mRNAs are modified to include only certain exons.
pre-mRNAs are produced by RNA polymerase using a template molecule.
tRNAs help add amino acids to a growing polypeptide chain.
rRNAs help translate mRNAs in the cytosol.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best represents how a single gene can encode more than one type of protein?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: A single gene can encode more than one type of protein because of a process called alternative splicing.

During alternative splicing, a single gene gives rise to more than one mature mRNA, each with a different combination of exons. In each diagram above, exons are represented by shaded boxes, and the top-most arrow(s) represent the transcription of a gene into mRNA. 

So, the diagram that best represents alternative splicing shows a single gene being transcribed into more than one mature mRNA, each with a different combination of exons:

[[☃ image 1]]

Each mRNA produced during alternative splicing can be translated into its corresponding protein. In the diagrams, the globular shapes represent proteins, and the bottom-most arrows represent the translation of mRNAs into these proteins. 

So, the diagram that best represents alternative splicing shows each mRNA being translated into its corresponding protein:

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct diagram is 

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A 3-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is split into two groups. The group on the left is the same assortment of rectangles from step 1. The group on the right only has 4 rectangles from step one, the black one has been removed.  All the connecting lines from step 1 are gone. In step 3 each of the groups from step 2 is converted into a separate globular shape consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in step 2 corresponds to the shading of each of the components in step 3.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c481b155eab724fe70927b729ac94b73.png)

![A 3-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is converted to a similar group of rectangles, but all the connecting lines are gone. In step 3 the group from step 2 is converted into two globular shapes consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in step 2 corresponds to the shading of the components in step 3.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/186fa465ff247ca73cf8db9930a51e93.png)

![A 3-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is split into two groups. The group on the left contains the same assortment of 5 rectangles from step 1. The group on the right has 4 rectangles from step one, but the black one has been removed.  All the connecting lines are gone in step 2. In step 3 both of the groups from step 2 are converted into a singular globular shape consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in step 2 corresponds to the shading of the components in step 3.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/abda572c0344a318c5ab7c5b22e50291.png)

![A 4-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is converted to a similar group of 5 rectangles, but all the connecting lines are gone. In step 3 the black rectangle is removed from the group from step 2 to create a new group. In step 4 the group from step 3 is converted into two globular shapes consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in step 2 corresponds to the shading of one of the components in step 4, and the shading of the rectangles in step 3 corresponds to the shading of the other component in step 4.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/faf4537aa08f097bac7f0b79f17ed7db.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the diagrams best represents alternative splicing, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: During alternative splicing, a single gene gives rise to more than one mature mRNA, each with a different combination of exons. Each of these mature mRNAs can then be translated into its corresponding protein.

In the diagrams above, the shaded boxes and lines represent exons and introns, respectively. This means that the figures made up of boxes and lines represent genes, and those made up of just boxes represent mature mRNAs. The globular shapes at the bottom of each diagram represent proteins.

So, Diagram A depicts a single gene giving rise to two mRNAs, each of which is translated into a protein. In contrast, Diagram B depicts a single gene giving rise to a single mRNA that is processed into a different mRNA, which is then translated into two proteins.
So, the correct answer is Diagram A, because it shows a single gene giving rise to two mRNAs, each of which is translated into a unique protein.

options.alt: Two diagrams, labeled Diagram A and Diagram B. Diagram A is a A 3-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is split into two groups. The group on the left shows the same assortment of rectangles from step 1. The group on the right shows 4 of the rectangles, the black one is missing. All the connecting lines are gone in step 2. In step 3 each of the groups from step 2 is converted into a separate globular shape consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in step 2 corresponds to the shading of the components in step 3. Diagram B is a A 4-step diagram. In step 1 a horizontal series of 5 rectangles with different shadings are connected by short lines. In step 2 this group of rectangles is converted to a similar group of rectangles, but all the connecting lines are gone. In step 3 the black rectangle is removed from the group from step 2 to create a new group. In step 4 the group from step 3 is converted into two globular shapes consisting of different components. The shading of the rectangles in the previous steps corresponds to the shading of the components in step 4.

choices.content: Diagram A, because it shows two genes, each with a unique set of exons, being translated into two proteins.
Diagram A, because it shows a single gene giving rise to two mRNAs, each of which is translated into a unique protein.
Diagram B, because it shows the processing of one mRNA into a different mRNA, which is translated into two proteins.
Diagram B, because it shows an exon being spliced out of an mRNA to give rise to two proteins.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which diagram best represents how a single gene can encode more than one type of protein, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: In the diagrams above, the shaded boxes and lines represent exons and introns, respectively. This means that the figures made up of boxes and lines represent genes, and those made up of just boxes represent mature mRNAs. The globular shapes at the bottom of each diagram represent proteins.

So, both diagrams show a process that results in a single gene giving rise to more than one type of protein. Diagram 1 shows a gene giving rise to one mature mRNA. In contrast, Diagram 2 shows a gene giving rise to two mature mRNAs, each with a different combination of exons, a process known as alternative splicing.
In alternative splicing, a single gene gives rise to more than one mature mRNA, each with a different combination of exons. Each mature mRNA can then be translated into its corresponding protein.
So, the correct answer is Diagram 2, because it shows the splicing together of exons to create more than one unique mRNA.

options.alt: Two side by side diagrams. Diagram 1 has 5 color-coded rectangular boxes connected by horizontal lines. The boxes are white, gray, black, off-white, and striped. An arrow points to the boxes connected to each other with the lines removed. There is a split arrow from the boxes that points to 2 separate structures. One structure has colored segments that are white, gray, black, off-white and striped. The other structure has colored segments that are white, gray, off white and striped. Diagram 2 has 5 color-coded rectangular boxes connected by horizontal lines. The boxes are white, gray, black, off-white and striped. A split arrow points to 2 different rows of connected boxes. The row on the left is white, gray, black, off-white and striped and from that row there is an arrow pointing to a structure with colored segments composed of the same colors. The row on the right is white, gray, off-white and striped and points to a structure with colored segments that are composed of the same colors.

choices.content: Diagram 1, because it shows the splicing together of exons to create more than one unique protein.
Diagram 1, because it shows two proteins arising from a single, unique mRNA.
Diagram 2, because it shows the translation of a single mRNA into more than one unique protein.
Diagram 2, because it shows the splicing together of exons to create more than one unique mRNA.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram represents the structure of the human *NFE2L2* gene. In the diagram, rectangles represent exons and lines represent introns.

[[☃ image 1]]

Mutations in the sequence of the *NFE2L2* gene have been associated with certain types of lung cancer. A group of scientists proposes that another cause of these cancers is the alternative splicing of *NFE2L2* pre-mRNAs. To test their claim, the scientists compare the sequence and abundance of mature *NFE2L2* mRNAs in normal lung tissue and cancerous lung tumors.

**Which of the following pieces of experimental evidence would best support the claim that alternative splicing of *NFE2L2* pre-mRNAs is associated with lung cancer in humans?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt: A segment of the NFE2L2 gene begins with a horizontal line, then a rectangle labeled 1, a horizontal line, a rectangle labeled 2, a horizontal line, a short rectangle labeled 3, a horizontal line, a rectangle labeled 4, a long horizontal line, and ends with a long rectangle labeled 5.

choices.content: *NFE2L2* mRNAs without Exon 2 are more common in tumors, while those with Exon 2 are more common in normal tissue.
*NFE2L2* mRNAs with Intron 4 are more common in tumors, while those without Intron 4 are more common in normal tissue.
*NFE2L2* mRNAs with and without Exon 3 are both present at high levels in tumors and in normal tissue.
*NFE2L2* mRNAs with and without a poly-A tail are both present at high levels in tumor tissue.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cystic fibrosis, a disease that causes sticky mucus to build up in a person’s organs, is caused by mutations in the *CFTR* gene. The structure of the *CFTR* gene is shown in the following diagram, where rectangles represent exons and horizontal lines represent introns.

[[☃ image 1]]

A group of scientists predicts that one of the mutations seen in cystic fibrosis patients causes alternative splicing of *CFTR* pre-mRNAs. To test their claim, the scientists compare the sequence and abundance of mature *CFTR* mRNAs in individuals with and without the mutation.

**Which of the following pieces of experimental evidence would best support the claim that alternative splicing of *CFTR* pre-mRNAs is occurring in individuals with the mutation described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Three segments of the CFTR gene are shown. Segment 1 is a small rectangular box labeled 1 followed by a horizontal line then a rectangular box labeled 2 then a horizontal line until the end of the segment. Segment 2 is a horizontal line then a rectangular box labeled 12, a horizontal line, a rectangular box labeled 13, a horizontal line, a long rectangular box labeled 14, a horizontal line, a rectangular box labeled 15, a horizontal line, a smaller rectangular box labeled 16 and then a horizontal line until the end of the segment. Segment 3 begins with a horizontal  line, then a rectangular box labeled 26, a horizontal line and ends with a smaller rectangular box labeled 27.

choices.content: *CFTR* mRNAs that exclude Exon 16 are more common in individuals with the mutation, while *CFTR* mRNAs that include Exon 16 are more common in individuals without the mutation.
*CFTR* mRNAs that include Intron 12 are more common in individuals with the mutation, while *CFTR* mRNAs that lack Intron 12 are more common in individuals without the mutation.
*CFTR* mRNAs with and without Exon 14 are both present at high levels in healthy individuals as well as in individuals with cystic fibrosis.
*CFTR* mRNAs with and without a poly-A tail are both present at high levels in healthy individuals as well as in individuals with cystic fibrosis.

assessment_items.question_content:
Schizophrenia is a brain condition that affects a person’s thinking, perceptions, and sense of self. This condition is associated with mutations in the *DRD2* gene. The structure of the *DRD2* gene is shown in the following diagram, where rectangles represent exons and horizontal lines represent introns.

[[☃ image 1]]

A group of scientists proposes that one of the mutations seen in patients with schizophrenia affects the alternative splicing of *DRD2* pre-mRNAs in the brain. To test their claim, the scientists compare the sequence and abundance of mature *DRD2* mRNAs in individuals with and without the mutation.

**Which of the following pieces of experimental evidence would best support the claim that alternative splicing of *DRD2* pre-mRNAs is associated with the mutation described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Two segments of the DRD2 gene are shown. Segment 1 is a rectangular box labeled 1 followed by a horizontal line until the end of the segment. Segment 2 is a horizontal line, a rectangular box labeled 2, a longer horizontal line leading to a rectangular box labeled 3, a horizontal line, a rectangular box labeled 4, a horizontal line, a rectangular box labeled 5, a horizontal line, a smaller rectangular box labeled 6, a horizontal line, a larger rectangular box labeled 7, a horizontal line, and ends with a large rectangular box labeled 8.

choices.content: *DRD2* mRNAs that include Exon 6 are more common in individuals with the mutation, while *DRD2* mRNAs that exclude Exon 6 are more common in individuals without the mutation.
*DRD2* mRNAs that include Intron 4 are more common in individuals without the mutation, while *DRD2* mRNAs that lack Intron 4 are more common in individuals with the mutation.
*DRD2* mRNAs with and without Exon 5 are present at equal levels in healthy individuals as well as in individuals with schizophrenia.
*DRD2* mRNAs with and without a 5’ cap are both present at high levels in healthy individuals as well as in individuals with schizophrenia.
